article_title: I gave Claude Code five years of notes and finally got the organization I'd been waiting for
core_thesis: The author argues that the fundamental problem with digital note-taking is not the app itself but the lack of an intelligent system that can read, understand, and reorganize messy notes. Using Claude Code (terminal version) to process five years of notes from AFFiNE and Windows Sticky Notes, they demonstrate that an AI assistant can effectively analyze, categorize, and restructure personal notes, turning a chaotic collection into a searchable, organized knowledge base. The key insight is that a 'reader' (AI) is more valuable than a 'container' (app) for note organization.
detailed_summary: The author describes their long struggle with digital note-taking across multiple apps (OneNote, Sticky Notes, Notion, AFFiNE), accumulating five years of disorganized notes. They realized that no app alone could fix the mess because notes are personal and require understanding of context. The solution was to use Claude Code (terminal version) to directly access and reorganize the local files. First, they gathered all notes into one folder: AFFiNE's local Markdown files were easy to copy, but Sticky Notes required manual extraction from a SQLite database (plum.sqlite) or manual copy-paste. After setting up Claude Code (installed via PowerShell command, requiring Git for Windows and PATH configuration), they launched it in the notes folder and asked it to analyze the contents. Claude created reader scripts to parse the AFFiNE SQLite database, then generated a five-phase checklist for organization. The author could customize the plan before execution. Claude then reorganized the notes, asking follow-up questions (e.g., whether to keep certain files). The result was a fully organized collection that could be used in AFFiNE, with the added benefit of Claude acting as a search engine for past thoughts. The author concludes that the problem was never the app but the need for an AI that can engage with content and make sense of it.

entities.name: Claude Code
entities.role: AI assistant used to analyze and reorganize notes

entities.name: AFFiNE
entities.role: Open-source note-taking app that stores notes locally as Markdown files

entities.name: Windows Sticky Notes
entities.role: Simple note-taking app whose notes were manually extracted

entities.name: Yadullah Abidi
entities.role: Author and computer science graduate with full-stack development and cybersecurity background

key_data_points: The author had five years of notes scattered across multiple apps (OneNote, Sticky Notes, Notion, AFFiNE), making organization a major challenge. 🟢 Relevant to your productivity: shows a real-world problem of note fragmentation that you may face as a student.

AFFiNE stores notes locally as Markdown files, making them easy to collect; Sticky Notes uses a SQLite database (plum.sqlite) at a specific Windows path, but the author had to manually copy notes because the folder didn't exist. 🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: understanding local data storage paths is useful for scripting backups or migrations.

Claude Code terminal version was installed via a single PowerShell command: 'curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.cmd -o install.cmd && install.cmd && del install.cmd'. Requires Git for Windows and PATH configuration. 🟢 Relevant to your programming: this is a concrete installation method you can replicate.

Claude Code automatically created reader scripts to access the AFFiNE SQLite database, then generated a five-phase checklist for organization. The user could customize the plan before execution. 🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: understanding how AI can parse local databases could be applied to data exfiltration or analysis in penetration testing.

After organization, Claude can act as a search engine for the notes, answering questions about past thoughts. 🟢 Relevant to your student needs: this turns notes into a queryable knowledge base, useful for studying and research.
methodology_steps: Step 1: Collect all notes into a single folder. For AFFiNE, copy the local data directory (Markdown files). For Sticky Notes, locate the plum.sqlite file at 'C:\Users\%USERNAME%\AppData\Local\Packages\Microsoft.MicrosoftStickyNotes_8wekyb3d8bbwe\LocalState' or manually copy each note into a text file.

Step 2: Install Claude Code terminal on Windows by running the PowerShell command: 'curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.cmd -o install.cmd && install.cmd && del install.cmd'. Ensure Git for Windows is installed and add Claude's install directory to the Windows PATH.
Step 3: Open a terminal in the folder containing all notes and run 'claude'. Log in with your Claude account.

Step 4: Ask Claude to analyze the folder contents. It will create reader scripts to parse databases (e.g., SQLite) and generate an organization plan (e.g., a five-phase checklist). Review and customize the plan as needed.
Step 5: Instruct Claude to execute the organization plan. It will ask follow-up questions (e.g., whether to keep certain files). Monitor the terminal and approve actions as prompted.
Step 6: After reorganization, use Claude as a search engine by asking questions about your notes. The organized notes can be re-imported into AFFiNE or another app.
conclusions: The core problem with note-taking is not the app but the lack of an intelligent system that can read and understand personal notes. AI assistants like Claude Code can fill this gap by analyzing content and reorganizing it meaningfully. This is supported by the author's successful reorganization of five years of notes in an afternoon.

Claude Code's ability to create custom scripts and ask follow-up questions makes it a flexible tool for note organization, but it requires user supervision and initial data preparation. The author emphasizes that the AI is not a replacement for clear thinking but a powerful assistant.

The approach also provides a secondary benefit: Claude becomes a search engine for your own thoughts, enabling quick retrieval of past ideas. This is particularly valuable for students and professionals who need to reference historical notes.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to you as a programmer and student because it demonstrates a practical, AI-driven method to organize years of messy notes—a common pain point. The technical steps (installing Claude Code, parsing local databases, scripting) align with your programming and DevOps interests, and the concept of using an AI to 'read' and reorganize data can inspire similar automation in your homelab or cybersecurity projects. As a student, the ability to turn notes into a searchable knowledge base can significantly boost your study efficiency. The article also reinforces the idea that tools alone aren't enough; you need a system that understands context, which is a valuable mindset for both coding and red teaming.
extra_advice: Consider extending this approach by writing a script that automatically exports notes from your current app (e.g., Obsidian, Notion) into a standardized Markdown format, then feeding them into Claude Code for periodic reorganization. You could also integrate this with a version control system (Git) to track changes over time, giving you a historical record of your thought evolution. For red teaming, experiment with using Claude Code to parse and analyze exfiltrated data (e.g., from a compromised machine) to quickly identify valuable information.
